data.title: Vita Coco shares set for another record as Q4, FY26 outlook beats expectations - update
data.description:
Updated 14:25 ET as shares surrendered opening gains to turn negative on EPS miss. 

Driven by high demand for its coconut water, Vita Coco (COCO) beat on nearly all financial metrics for the fourth quarter and set an outlook for FY26 that was above Wall Street’s expectations.

Update: After reaching what would be a record high during regular hours, shares reversed course at the open and drifted lower throughout the day, losing as much as 10%.

The retreat is blamed on an atypical EPS miss, the first for the company in 12 quarters. 

"The coconut water category continues to be one of the fastest growing beverage categories, and Vita Coco Coconut Water retail sales are growing at healthy rates in the United States and our core international markets,” said Vita Coco CEO Michael Kirban. “I believe this growth is being driven by our investments as the category leader, which is resulting in increased household penetration and new consumption occasions. I believe we are well positioned to continue to drive growth based on category momentum, the strength of the Vita Coco brand, and the performance of our supply chain.”

During the fourth quarter, net sales increased 0.4% to $128M during the quarter as strong coconut water sales were partially offset by softness in the company’s private label, though still beat estimates by $9M. By region, sales in The Americas were down 6.4%, while international sales were up 42%, both of which also beat expectations.

The company’s bottom line improved as well as gross profit improved 8% to $44.6M versus $40.7M, and adjusted EBITDA was as much as 83% higher than a year ago at $14.1M, beating estimates by $4M.

On a per share basis, however, the company’s results came in short of Wall Street’s expectations, as adjusted EPS of $0.09 increased by 3 cents from last year but missed $0.13 estimates. For the year, the company’s adjusted profit improved 27% to $1.19 per share.

Gross margin expanded 300 basis points to 35% in Q4 and is expected to increase another 100 basis points for FY26 to 38% from 37% in FY25. 

The company’s balance sheet remained strong as well, with no debt and cash and cash equivalents of $197M, up 19% from the beginning of the year. Inventory levels increased 32% to $111M, and accounts receivable improved 30% to $82M.

Looking ahead to the full year, Vita Coco expects sales to be within a range of $680M and $700M, with a midpoint of $690M that beats $684.4M estimates.

Adjusted EBITDA is seen between $122M and $128M versus $121.7M estimates.

data.title: Empire State Realty Trust signals 2026 FFO guidance of $0.85–$0.89 per share while advancing NYC portfolio transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO Anthony Malkin opened by highlighting the company's continued leasing momentum, observation deck execution, balance sheet management, and the outlook for 2026. He stated, "We delivered full year core FFO of $0.87, a reflection of continued performance across our platform. Our leasing team again put points on the board with nearly 460,000 square feet leased in the quarter and 1 million square feet for the year." Malkin emphasized the transformation to a 100% New York City portfolio, with $1 billion in acquisitions and the disposal of suburban commercial assets without tax leakage. He underscored management succession and key leadership promotions, stating these have "strengthened our operating platform and reinforce our ability to execute on our growth initiatives."
  • President Christina Chiu detailed recent investment activity, noting, "In December, we acquired 130 Mercer for $386 million. Our ability to move quickly and close with certainty is a significant advantage in today's market." She reported completion of $417 million in all-cash acquisitions and the final disposition of Metro Center. Chiu also highlighted share repurchases: "During the fourth quarter, we repurchased $6 million of shares at an average price of $6.73. For the full year, we repurchased $8 million of shares at an average price of $6.78."
  • Executive VP Ryan Kass reported, "In 2025, our property team delivered another year of exceptional performance. We leased over 1 million square feet and grew occupancy to 90.3%, up 170 basis points year-over-year. Our office portfolio is 93.5% leased, our 12th consecutive quarter above 90%." Kass noted, "The fourth quarter marked our 18th consecutive quarter of positive mark-to-market lease spreads in our office portfolio."
  • CFO Stephen Horn presented, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, we reported core FFO of $0.23 per diluted share. For the full year 2025, core FFO was $0.87 per diluted share. Same-store property cash NOI, excluding lease termination fees, increased 3.4% year-over-year for the fourth quarter."

Outlook

  • CFO Horn stated, "At a high level, we expect 2026 FFO and same-store cash NOI to be consistent with our 2025 results."
  • Horn provided explicit guidance: "We expect core FFO to range from $0.85 to $0.89 per diluted share. Our guidance assumes same-store property cash NOI growth of negative 1.5% to positive 2%." Occupancy is expected at 90% to 92% by year-end 2026, compared to 90.3% at year-end 2025.
  • For the Observatory, "We expect 2026 NOI of approximately $87 million to $92 million and expenses of approximately $10 million per quarter."
  • G&A for calendar year 2026 is guided to aggregate approximately $69 million to $71 million, with a planned reduction in run rate G&A by 5% to 10% by year-end 2026.

Financial Results

  • CFO Horn reported, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, we reported core FFO of $0.23 per diluted share. For the full year 2025, core FFO was $0.87 per diluted share."
  • Same-store property cash NOI increased 3.4% year-over-year for the quarter. Excluding nonrecurring items, same-store cash revenue increased 2.5% and operating expenses increased 1.7% in the fourth quarter.
  • The Observatory business generated approximately $24 million of NOI in the fourth quarter and $90 million for the full year. Revenue per capita increased 6.9% year-over-year in the fourth quarter.
  • FAD CapEx reduced by approximately $21 million or 11% year-over-year, with the primary contributor being an $18 million reduction in CapEx spent on building improvements.

Q&A

  • Manas Abic, Evercore ISI, asked about leasing momentum into 2026. Kass responded, "We continue to see a bifurcated market of the have and have-nots and where we have. We have just over 170,000 square feet of leases in the pipeline that we anticipate closing in the first and second quarter."
  • John Kim, BMO Capital Markets, inquired about the Metro Center sale and ability to pass property tax increases. Chiu replied, "It was mid-$60 million. And then with some credit and adjustment, it gets to right around the debt balance. And from an NOI perspective, it's around [ 7 ] cap rate." Malkin and Kass confirmed that property tax increases would be passed through on a tax escalation to tenants.
  • Kim further asked about the impact of AI on leasing. Malkin stated, "AI itself has been a positive for the leasing market and the source of incremental tenant demand... At this point, we're more hindered by the availability of space to lease than anything else."
  • Nicholas Joseph, Citi, asked about New York City policy impacts. Kass answered, "It has not impacted any of our leasing discussions." Malkin added, "We just do what we can do here at ESRT, got a balance sheet that allows us to do our business... and we will make money where we can make money."
  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo, asked for more detail on occupancy forecasts. Kass said, "We feel very confident in our year-end occupancy guidance of that 90% to 92%." Chiu addressed leverage, "We've also said that from time to time, we may tick up on net debt to EBITDA, it's not a strict limit... when there are great opportunities... you may pick up from time to time, and we'll have a game plan as we navigate going forward."
  • Dylan Burzinski, Green Street, asked about World Cup impacts on Observatory demand. Malkin replied, "We're optimistic it will benefit our business with the biggest upside really around co-branding opportunities... but we don't rely on just one event."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, requesting clarification on leasing pipelines, asset sales, and tax pass-throughs, with limited skepticism evidenced.
  • Management’s prepared remarks and responses remained confident throughout, with Malkin and Chiu frequently emphasizing the company’s strong balance sheet and operational flexibility. Malkin used phrases such as, "We are confident in our position as we look ahead," and "We just do what we can do here at ESRT." During Q&A, the tone remained steady, with no significant defensive or evasive responses.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a similarly confident and pragmatic tone, with no major shifts in sentiment or concerns.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Core FFO for the fourth quarter remained unchanged at $0.23 per share, consistent with the previous quarter. Full year 2025 core FFO was reported at $0.87 per share.
  • Occupancy increased from 90.3% to 93.5% leased in the office portfolio, maintaining a trend of high occupancy and positive mark-to-market lease spreads.
  • Guidance for 2026 FFO ($0.85–$0.89 per share) and same-store cash NOI (negative 1.5% to positive 2%) reflects a stable outlook, with only a minor anticipated impact from known tenant rollovers such as FDIC.
  • Capital allocation activity accelerated with the acquisition of 130 Mercer for $386 million and continued share repurchases.
  • Management sentiment and confidence remained strong, with ongoing focus on disciplined capital allocation and operational execution. Analysts’ tone continued neutral to slightly positive, with limited concern beyond the standard clarifications.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the impact of the FDIC vacancy at Empire State Building, which will create temporary downtime affecting 2026 core FFO by approximately $0.03 per share and reducing same-store property NOI growth by 270 basis points.
  • Operating expense growth is expected to be driven by higher real estate taxes and labor costs, partially offset by tenant reimbursements.
  • Market volatility, capital market uncertainty, and potential policy changes in New York City were noted, but management emphasized proactive balance sheet management and operational flexibility as mitigation strategies.
  • Analysts raised concerns on potential property tax impacts, leverage metrics, and the broader market environment, all of which management addressed with detailed responses.

Final Takeaway

Empire State Realty Trust enters 2026 with a fully transitioned New York City portfolio, continued leasing strength, and a solid balance sheet. Management projects stable core FFO and NOI for the coming year despite temporary headwinds from tenant rollover and market volatility. Strategic acquisitions, disciplined capital allocation, and operational excellence remain central, positioning the company to drive long-term value for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon says he now owns Bitcoin
data.description:
Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon, long known as a cryptocurrency skeptic, disclosed on Wednesday that he now personally holds “very, very limited” amounts of Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Speaking at the World Liberty Forum at Mar-a-Lago in Florida, Solomon characterized himself as merely an “observer” of the digital asset rather than a “great Bitcoin (BTC-USD) prognosticator.”

While he now admits to owning “very little, but some” of Bitcoin (BTC-USD), he noted he is still trying to understand how the asset moves. He previously said he didn’t see “a real use case” for the cryptocurrency, noting that he always considered it to be a speculative investment.

Solomon used his appearance to articulate a vision where traditional finance and cryptocurrency are not adversaries but components of a unified system.

“It’s one system; it’s our system,” he said. “We have to do it the right way.”

He emphasized that tokenization will be “super important” as large-scale technology platforms continue to reshape market infrastructure.

The Goldman Sachs chief attributed his firm’s historically cautious approach to the digital asset space to regulatory constraints. Solomon suggested that as regulators begin providing greater latitude for companies to get “more involved” in the sector, the investment banking company may take another look at its participation level.

The firm’s leadership views crypto as part of a longer-term shift in financial infrastructure, a perspective that has gained increased legitimacy under President Donald Trump’s administration. Solomon urged a thoughtful approach even as the regulatory environment appears to be evolving rapidly in favor of greater institutional involvement.

Bitcoin-backed ETFs: (IBIT), (ARKB), (GBTC), (BRRR), (BTCO), (HODL), (BTCW), (FBTC), (BITB), (EZBC)

data.title: Economy makes progress on inflation, labor market stabilizes, but uncertainty lingers: FOMC minutes
data.description:
Federal Reserve officials largely agreed that the U.S. economy was expanding solidly, consumer spending has been resilient, the unemployment rate has held steady in recent months, and they generally expect inflation to continue declining in 2026, according to the minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's Jan. 27-28 meeting.

Recall that the Federal Open Market Committee voted to keep the federal funds rate target range unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% at its last meeting, following three straight rate cuts in late 2025. The decision prompted dissents from Fed Governors Stephen Miran and Christopher Waller, who preferred to cut the rate by 25 basis points.

While it appears the Fed is making progress on inflation moving toward its 2% goal, many members remained cautious. 

"Most participants, however, cautioned that progress toward the committee's 2% objective might be slower and more uneven than generally expected and judged that the risk of inflation running persistently above the committee's objective was meaningful," the minutes said.

The majority of FOMC members agreed that downside risks to the labor market had diminished, but they're not yet willing to sound the “all clear” signal. "While participants generally assessed that, under appropriate monetary policy, the labor market likely would stabilize and then improve this year, they continued to note that the outlook for the labor market remained uncertain," the summary of the meeting said.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: Genius Sports rallies after its CEO defends the recent Legend acquisition
data.description:
Genius Sports Limited (GENI) jumped more than 10% in afternoon trading on Wednesday after CEO Mark Locke issued a letter to shareholders defending the decision of the company to acquire Legend. The acquisition contributed to some selling pressure on Genius Sports (GENI) since the announcement date of February 5.

Locke noted that some people think that Genius Sports (GENI) bought a simple affiliate business. "Our view is different. We bought a participation layer built on two decades of technological investment that sits between official data infrastructure and the moment of transaction," he highlighted. Importantly, Locke said Legend is not simply a media business but a technology company built around large, loyal sports and iGaming audiences. 

Locke said across brands such as Covers.com, Casino.org, and Casino Guru, Legend generated 320M visits from 118M unique users in 2025, of which more than two-thirds of those users return regularly. Genius Sports (GENI) sees the real value behind the Legend audience scale as the technology and behavioral intelligence that powers how that audience participates.

"We are paying just over six times pre-earnout EBITDA for a business with global scale, strong cash flow, and its proprietary ad-tech stack and audience data built on over 20 years and $300 million investment in technology and advanced data modeling purpose-built for sports and gaming," Locke added.

Genius Sports (GENI) is well liked on Wall Street, with 15 out of 16 analysts covering the name in the bull camp. Seeking Alpha analysts also have a consensus Buy rating on the sports data stock.

data.title: NIH director reportedly being tapped as acting CDC director
data.description:
The director of the National Institutes of Health ((NIH)), Jay Bhattacharya, is expected to take on an additional role as the acting director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention ((CDC)), the New York Times reported, citing two government officials.

While remaining as the head of the NIH, Bhattacharya will lead the CDC until President Donald Trump names a permanent replacement, which requires Senate confirmation.

The appointment comes after Jim O’Neill, the former acting CDC director, resigned last week as part of a leadership shake-up at the Department of Health and Human Services ((HHS)).

O’Neill, who also left his position as deputy health secretary, is expected to be nominated by Trump to lead the National Science Foundation, according to the officials.

In August, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. fired former CDC director Susan Monarez less than a month after she took office, accusing her of not aligning with the Trump administration’s healthcare policies.

data.title: Prog Holdings outlines $3B–$3.1B 2026 revenue target as multiproduct platform accelerates growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PROG Holdings (PRG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Steven Michaels, CEO, opened the call by highlighting that "2025 was a year that required balance, discipline and focus," noting that the company navigated a challenging retail and consumer environment and the bankruptcy of a large retail partner. He reported that leasing GMV was down 8.6% year-over-year, but after adjusting for the Big Lots bankruptcy and intentional tightening, underlying GMV grew in the mid-single digits. Michaels emphasized that "Four delivered triple-digit GMV and revenue growth throughout the year" and is playing an "increasingly important role in our ecosystem."
  • Michaels detailed that Money App and Four contributed approximately $45 million of incremental leasing GMV in 2025, up from $23 million the previous year. The sale of the Vive portfolio was described as a strategic move to redeploy capital toward areas with stronger alignment and return potential. Michaels also announced the acquisition of Purchasing Power, completed in January 2026, expanding offerings into a new channel and growth platform.
  • Michaels introduced Lee Wright as the new President of Purchasing Power, highlighting his background in retail and consumer finance and noting, "We're excited to have him on the team as we integrate the business and unlock its long-term potential."
  • Brian Garner, CFO, stated, "Q4 consolidated revenues were within our outlook range provided in October and our adjusted EBITDA of $61.5 million along with a non-GAAP EPS at $0.74 exceeded the high end of this outlook." He added, "Consolidated gross margins improved 284 basis points to 36.3%, driven by margin expansion at Progressive Leasing and a shift towards higher-margin Four revenue."

Outlook

  • The company expects 2026 consolidated revenues to be in the range of $3 billion to $3.1 billion, with adjusted EBITDA targeted at $320 million to $350 million and non-GAAP EPS in the range of $4 to $4.45. Purchasing Power is projected to contribute $680 million to $730 million in revenue and $50 million to $60 million of adjusted EBITDA for the full year. Management stated, "This outlook assumes a difficult operating environment with soft demand for consumer durable goods, no material changes in the company's decisioning posture, an effective tax rate for non-GAAP EPS of approximately 26%, no material increases in the unemployment rate for our consumer and no impact from additional share repurchases."
  • Michaels noted, "We plan for an operating environment that remains challenging," but also sees "higher expected tax refunds in 2026 should provide incremental liquidity and near-term support for demand and repayment behavior."

Financial Results

  • Q4 consolidated revenues from continuing operations were reported as $574.6 million. Q4 adjusted EBITDA was $61.5 million, and non-GAAP EPS was $0.74. For the full year, consolidated adjusted EBITDA from continuing operations was $269 million and non-GAAP diluted EPS was $3.51. Michaels highlighted that "PROG Marketplace expanded meaningfully in 2025, delivering approximately $82 million in GMV, nearly doubling year-over-year."
  • Progressive Leasing's Q4 revenue was $545 million, an 8.1% year-over-year decline, and fourth quarter GMV declined 10.6%, primarily due to the Big Lots bankruptcy and intentional tightening. Gross margin expanded by approximately 90 basis points, and write-offs were 7.6% of revenue in Q4, within the targeted range.
  • Four generated approximately $736 million of GMV in 2025, representing 144% growth year-over-year, and delivered approximately $10 million of adjusted EBITDA for the full year.

Q&A

  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens Inc.: Asked about Purchasing Power performance. Michaels responded, "That revenue outlook implies a low double-digit revenue growth for the business" and targets a 7% to 8% adjusted EBITDA margin.
  • Joseph: Inquired about credit outlook by product. Garner answered, "We're encouraged by what we're seeing thus far in terms of the outcome of our tightening efforts a year ago" and expects improving EBITDA margins for Four, targeting just over 15% for 2026.
  • Joseph: Asked about the evolution of the sales process with multiple products. Michaels explained, "We're incorporating Four and Money app into our discussions as it relates to our existing relationships."
  • Harold Goetsch, B. Riley Securities: Queried about Purchasing Power ABS transactions and cost of capital. Michaels said, "We expect that we can improve on those economics, but we can't commit to anything until the deal is closed."
  • Goetsch: Asked about BNPL segment growth. Michaels attributed strong performance to a smaller base and highlighted, "Our Four Plus subscription service is getting wide acceptance and actually exceeding our expectations as it relates to active subscribers."
  • Bradley Thomas, KeyBanc: Asked about GMV softness. Michaels noted, "It was a weakish October, weakish first 3 weeks of November and then a slight rebound into the holidays and into -- through Christmas."
  • Alessandra Jimenez, Raymond James: Sought clarity on synergy opportunities from Purchasing Power. Michaels identified "accelerate the growth rate of the business" as the largest opportunity, with scale and penetration into existing eligibles.
  • Anthony Chukumba, Loop Capital: Asked about a $5 billion write-off. Michaels clarified, "You have to take the unamortized portion of those economics through the P&L" when a retail partner goes into liquidation.
  • Hoang Nguyen, TD Cowen: Questioned the strong EPS guidance. Michaels detailed, "Purchasing Power, we've said we believe it's a double-digit accretive acquisition for 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on integration, profitability outlook for new segments, and credit discipline, while seeking clarity on margin drivers and synergy realization.
  • Management maintained a confident stance during prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and resilience, but demonstrated a more measured and occasionally cautious tone in response to Q&A, particularly when discussing macro headwinds and integration challenges. Michaels frequently referenced strategic discipline and portfolio health as ongoing priorities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained consistently constructive but more focused on the impact of acquisitions and the path to margin improvement. Management's tone shifted from highlighting resilience and outperformance in Q3 to a more forward-looking, integration-focused, and cautiously optimistic approach in Q4.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The 2026 revenue outlook of $3 billion to $3.1 billion represents a significant step up from the 2025 guidance. The addition of Purchasing Power and the continued strong performance of Four mark a strategic expansion from the prior quarter, which focused more on disciplined execution and portfolio health amid challenging conditions.
  • Q3's sentiment centered on navigating headwinds and the sale of Vive, while Q4 focused on integration, synergy, and scaling the multiproduct platform. Analysts shifted attention from portfolio resilience and macro stress in Q3 to questions around the impact of new products, synergy capture, and margin expansion in Q4.
  • Management's confidence in the multiproduct ecosystem and cross-selling opportunities increased, while maintaining caution around persistent macro challenges and integration risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistent consumer headwinds, particularly in durable goods and discretionary categories, and acknowledged the revenue impact from a smaller lease portfolio entering 2026.
  • Integration risks for Purchasing Power and the need to realize synergies were discussed, with Michaels noting, "It's not going to be straight up into the right as it never is with integrating an acquisition."
  • Analysts expressed concerns about credit performance across new segments, seasonality, and the sustainability of GMV growth rates, particularly in the BNPL segment.
  • Management outlined a disciplined approach to portfolio management, cost controls, and ongoing monitoring of macroeconomic pressures.

Final Takeaway

PROG Holdings emphasized a transformative year highlighted by strategic divestiture, a major acquisition, and robust expansion of its multiproduct platform. Management projects 2026 consolidated revenues of $3 billion to $3.1 billion, with strong contributions expected from Purchasing Power and continued momentum in Four. The company remains focused on disciplined execution, integration of new assets, and leveraging cross-product engagement to drive long-term, profitable growth, while maintaining vigilance on macro challenges and portfolio health.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elliott Management's ambitious goal for Norwegian Cruise Line faces major hurdles—Citi Research
data.description:
Elliott Management recently disclosed a 10% stake in Norwegian Cruise Lines (NCLH) in what will likely be a campaign by the activist investor to effect changes in the company’s board and reverse years of mismanagement.

In a letter to shareholders, Elliott blamed NCLH’s underperformance on the board’s lack of expertise, a new CEO without industry experience, and bloated costs that were misaligned with competitors.

As part of its effort to realign the business and return to profitable growth, Elliott targets $4B+ in EBITDA by 2027 versus street estimates for 2027 EBITDA of $3.35B.

Citi Research analyst James Hardiman thinks this goal is unachievable by 2027 and that the required turnaround outlined by the activist investor “presents significant challenges.”

First off, Norwegian (NCLH) will need to face the challenge of fighting a best-in-class and better-capitalized Royal Caribbean (RCL) for essentially the same customer.

“This does not excuse NCLH’s performance (rather, it underscores the importance of getting their house in order) but speaks to the difficulty of regaining ground overnight,” Hardiman says.

Second, cost performance has been getting better but may not be good enough. Hardiman is encouraged by the significant improvement of the trajectory of unit costs over the past couple of years, but the cuts have come off an “exceedingly high base” that occurred during the “cost is no object” tenure of previous CEO Frank Del Rio.

And finally, private destination investments are not a panacea for NCLH, as the company is already behind its competitors. 

“There is no easy fix here, as even after the opening of Great Stirrup Cay’s waterpark, NCLH will likely find itself losing ground against RCLH, who will have 4+ land assets by 2027,” Hardiman adds.

While $4B+ EBITDA in 2027 seems unlikely, Hardiman thinks that with just modest margin improvements, significantly better execution, “and maybe most of all a steadier hand,” NCLH could reach that goal by 2028, “on the strength of which we could easily argue for a 1x multiple premium.”

data.title: Charles River projects up to 9% EPS growth in 2026 as new CEO, acquisitions, and cost savings drive margin expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Charles River Laboratories International, Inc. (CRL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • James Foster, Chairman, President & CEO, stated that 2025 results landed at the upper end of the revenue and non-GAAP EPS guidance, highlighting improvements in DSA net bookings and a stabilized biopharma demand environment. Strategic initiatives were advanced, including acquisitions and a renewed focus on scientific innovation, positioning Charles River as a leader in preclinical drug development.
  • Foster reported, "We were pleased that our 2025 financial results were at the upper end of the revenue and non-GAAP earnings per share ranges that we provided in November." He also announced the acquisitions of K.F. (Cambodia) and PathoQuest, which are expected to enhance the company’s supply chain and testing capabilities.
  • DSA net book-to-bill improved to 1.1x in the fourth quarter, with biotech clients driving a steady, sequential increase. Foster indicated cautious optimism for continued favorable demand trends into 2026.
  • Foster shared, "We also continue to make progress on our plan to divest businesses totaling approximately 7% of 2025 annual revenue. These processes and negotiations with potential buyers are ongoing and we continue to expect the planned divestitures will be completed by middle of 2026."
  • Foster announced his retirement effective in May and introduced Birgit Girshick as the incoming CEO.
  • Birgit Girshick, Corporate Executive VP & COO, provided 2026 guidance and discussed the expectation for organic revenue to range from down 1% to at least flat, with operating margin improvement of 20 to 50 basis points. "This is expected to translate into non-GAAP earnings per share in a range from $10.70 to $11.20, representing growth of approximately 4% to 9%," Girshick stated.
  • Girshick also announced that Glenn Coleman will join as CFO in April and Kerry Dailey as Chief Legal Officer in March.
  • Michael Knell, Interim CFO, explained, "On a reported basis, we expect revenue will be between at least flat and 1.5% growth... We also expect a small revenue benefit from PathoQuest once the acquisition closes later this quarter."

Outlook

  • Guidance for 2026 sets organic revenue in a range from down 1% to at least flat, with operating margin expected to improve by 20 to 50 basis points from 2025’s 19.8%.
  • Non-GAAP earnings per share are projected between $10.70 and $11.20, with K.F. expected to add about $0.25 to EPS this year.
  • Segment outlooks include RMS revenue declining at a low to mid-single digit rate, DSA revenue in a range between slightly positive and a low single-digit decrease, and Manufacturing rebounding to a low single-digit increase. Microbial Solutions is expected to sustain mid-single digit growth.
  • Most of the EPS improvement in 2026 is expected from operations, with at least $100 million in incremental cost savings above 2025’s level.
  • The first quarter of 2026 is expected to be pressured by unfavorable RMS mix, higher stock compensation due to CEO transition, and higher DSA costs, but margin improvement is expected after Q1.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $994.2 million, a 2.6% organic decline year-over-year, with decreases in all three business segments.
  • Full-year 2025 revenue was $4.02 billion, down 1.6% organically, primarily from lower DSA and Manufacturing revenue.
  • Operating margin for Q4 declined 180 basis points year-over-year to 18.1%, and full-year margin was 19.8%.
  • Q4 EPS was $2.39, a decrease from $2.66 in Q4 2024. Full-year EPS was $10.28, nearly flat to 2024.
  • DSA revenue in Q4 was $591.6 million, down 3.3% organically. DSA operating margin was 20.1% in Q4 and 24.2% for the year.
  • RMS revenue in Q4 was $206.3 million, down 0.9% organically; Manufacturing Solutions revenue was $196.4 million, down 2.1% organically.
  • Free cash flow for 2026 is expected in the range of $375 million to $400 million, with capital expenditures of approximately $200 million.

Q&A

  • Eric Coldwell, Baird: Questioned NHP volume discrepancies between RMS and DSA. Birgit Girshick: Explained differences were due to timing and sourcing, with higher open market costs in DSA and shipment timing impacting RMS.
  • Luke Sergott, Barclays: Asked about DSA hiring and AI risks. James Foster: Indicated capacity is sufficient and AI is seen as an enabling technology, not a disruptor, stating, "AI is a NAMs, and we're focused on NAMs to the extent that the science is beneficial, the science is additive."
  • Christine Rains, William Blair: Asked about DSA cancellations. Foster: Noted cancellations are back to normal levels and manageable.
  • David Windley, Jefferies: Asked about client urgency and demand stability. Foster: Stated, "Demand is improving from a whole host of factors," with backlog at a healthy level and more balanced demand emerging.
  • Charles Rhyee, TD Cowen: Inquired about margin improvement trajectory. Michael Knell: Cited cost savings, K.F. acquisition, and demand improvement as key drivers for sequential margin gains.
  • Elizabeth Anderson, Evercore: Asked about China RMS demand and biologics testing. Foster: Reported China RMS growth and biologics testing recovery in Q4.
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA: Questioned DSA demand volatility and confidence in continued improvement. Foster: Attributed prior volatility to biotech funding constraints, expressing cautious optimism as funding rebounds.
  • Casey Woodring, JPMorgan: Asked about capital deployment and NHP sourcing. Foster: Indicated NHP sourcing is secure and capital allocation remains disciplined.
  • Justin Bowers, Deutsche Bank: Sought clarity on client adoption of NAMs. Foster: Reported diverse adoption across client base, especially in big pharma and discovery.
  • Patrick Donnelly, Citi: Requested update on divestitures. Foster: Confirmed process is ongoing, aiming for completion in first half of 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed persistent curiosity about demand stability, margin recovery, and the impact of AI and NAMs, with a slightly positive to neutral tone, often pressing for specifics on guidance, costs, and implementation of strategic initiatives.
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, but responses in Q&A were measured and cautiously optimistic, frequently reiterating caution about linearity in demand and margin trends. Phrases such as "cautiously optimistic" and "trying not to overcall it" were common.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management showed increased confidence about margin expansion and cost control, while analysts shifted from concern about bookings to focus on execution of new strategies and M&A.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a focus on stabilization to cautious optimism for growth in the second half of 2026.
  • Strategic focus moved from cost-saving and portfolio review to execution of acquisitions and leadership transition.
  • Key metrics such as DSA net book-to-bill and segment revenue were more positive in Q4, and management highlighted the benefit of the K.F. acquisition.
  • Analysts' tone evolved from skepticism about DSA growth to probing around the specifics and timing of guidance targets, margin recovery, and M&A pipeline.
  • Management’s confidence increased regarding demand drivers, cost savings, and strategic priorities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing market volatility, particularly in biotech funding and RMS demand from small models in North America.
  • Higher NHP sourcing costs and timing of shipments remain near-term risks, but are expected to normalize with the K.F. acquisition.
  • Analysts raised concerns around AI disruption, margin pressure in Q1 2026, and execution of planned divestitures.
  • Management emphasized ongoing cost controls, strategic acquisitions, and disciplined capital deployment to mitigate risks.

Final Takeaway

Charles River Laboratories enters 2026 with a new CEO, a stronger supply chain through recent acquisitions, and a disciplined approach to cost savings and capital deployment. The company expects margin expansion and non-GAAP EPS growth of 4% to 9%, supported by operational improvements and favorable demand trends. While near-term headwinds persist, particularly in the first quarter, management remains cautiously optimistic about a return to organic revenue growth in the second half of the year and is focused on capturing new opportunities for long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BofA launches new loyalty program for banking, investing account holders
data.description:
Bank of America (BAC) on Wednesday introduced a new loyalty program to increase client engagement.

The no-fee program, called BofA Rewards, is available to any client with a BofA personal checking account, requires no minimum balance, and is "designed to reward and recognize clients for their full relationship across their Bank of America banking and Merrill investing accounts," the company said.

Starting on May 27, millions of clients will be eligible for the program that offers such benefits as credit cards, cash-back deals, banking services, and curated experiences. Based on clients' membership tier and program engagement, the BofA Rewards benefits can range from $150 to $4,000 in annual value per person.

Bank of America (BAC) said more than 30M existing clients are currently eligible for the program. While there's no minimum balance to join the program, balance amounts do determine a client's membership tier — Member tier: less than $30K; Preferred Plus tier: $30K to less than $100K; Preferred Honors tier: $100K to less than $1M; and Premier tier: $1M and above.

The program is meant to provide reward offerings to recognize clients' goals at every life stage, BofA said. Rewards include credit card reward bonuses of 10%-75% on everyday purchases with eligible credit cards, cash-back deals on more than 15K brands, exclusive discounts on home and auto loans, and enhanced fraud and identity monitoring.

Preferred Honors and and Premier tier members can get reimbursed for popular subscriptions from streaming, entertainment, and news services, with credits of up to $96 per year for Preferred Honors and up to $180 per year for Premier tier members.

Bank of America (BAC) stock rose 1.0% in Wednesday afternoon trading.
